s1_reasoning: The dominant computation is the stencil operation in compute_tran_temp() which performs floating-point arithmetic on every grid cell for each iteration. Memory access patterns are regular (sequential array accesses), but the arithmetic operations (multiplications, additions, divisions) dominate execution time. The ratio of arithmetic operations to memory accesses is high (~10 FLOPs per grid cell per iteration).

hotspots.reason: Triple nested loops performing stencil computation on 2D grid with many conditional branches for boundary handling
Most grid cells are interior (512x512 grid = 262,144 cells, 99.6% interior), executing the same arithmetic operations repeatedly
Initialization of large arrays with random values, memory-bound but only executed once

bottleneck_type.reasoning: The dominant computation is the stencil operation in compute_tran_temp() which performs floating-point arithmetic on every grid cell for each iteration. Memory access patterns are regular (sequential array accesses), but the arithmetic operations (multiplications, additions, divisions) dominate execution time. The ratio of arithmetic operations to memory accesses is high (~10 FLOPs per grid cell per iteration).

gpu_suitability.reasoning: The computation is highly parallelizable with regular data access patterns. Each grid cell can be updated independently per iteration (except for neighbor dependencies within the same iteration, which are read-only). The problem size (512x512 = 262K cells) is sufficient to saturate GPU cores.
gpu_suitability.recommended_approach: Port to CUDA or OpenCL with 2D thread blocks. Each thread processes one grid cell. Use shared memory for tile-based computation to reduce global memory accesses. Implement kernel fusion to combine boundary conditions. Use texture memory for power array if it's read-only.
